Most of the chemical elements in our bodies exist in the form of compounds which can be further classified as *inorganic compounds* (lacking carbon) or *organic compounds* (containing carbon). Some of the most important organic compounds that are relevant to sport and exercise metabolism include carbohydrates, fats and proteins. Collectively, these compounds are called the *macronutrients*, and it is through the action of specific biochemical processes (i.e. chemical reactions) that our bodies utilize these food sources to provide our muscles with the energy to exercise. Figure 3.3 shows the general flow of chemical organization from atom to macronutrient.

## Definition

Chemical compounds in the human body are substances formed from chemical elements, classified as *inorganic compounds* (lacking carbon) or *organic compounds* (containing carbon). The macronutrients—carbohydrates, fats, and proteins—are the principal organic compounds relevant to sport and exercise metabolism.

## Core Principles

- Chemical elements in the body combine to form compounds, partitioned into inorganic (no carbon) and organic (carbon-containing) categories.
- Macronutrients (carbohydrates, fats, proteins) are organic compounds utilized by the body through biochemical reactions to supply energy for muscular work.
- Chemical organization proceeds in a hierarchy from atoms → molecules/compounds → macronutrients (see Figure 3.3).

## Sub-concepts

- Inorganic compounds (lacking carbon)
- Organic compounds (containing carbon)
- Macronutrients: carbohydrates, fats, proteins
- Biochemical processes / chemical reactions linking food sources to muscle energy supply

## Field-level Controversies

## 文獻分歧 / Discussion

## Practical Applications

- Understanding the classification of body compounds underpins sport and exercise nutrition: dietary intake of carbohydrates, fats, and proteins provides the substrates that biochemical reactions convert into usable energy for exercising muscle.
